attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 6 kg. Over the past three weeks he has noticed a dusky discolouration over his upper chest and neck (Image). He reports a sense of facial fullness when bending forward but no chest pain, cough, haemoptysis or fevers. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and drinks four glasses of wine daily. He lives independently and has not travelled recently.

On examination, he appears thin with the visible changes as shown in the photograph. His pulse rate is 84/min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. The physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. No initial investigations have yet been performed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most obvious changes to be seen are the dilated veins on the upper chest and the prominent inferior and superior epigastric veins. These are signs associated with superior and inferior vena caval obstruction. These veins are most likely to be obstructed either by blood clot or external compression. The latter might be due to compression by a primary tumour or lymph node enlargement. The patient's report of facial fullness when bending forward is a classic symptom of superior vena cava syndrome, occurring when venous return from the head and upper body is impaired and worsens with positional changes that increase intrathoracic pressure.

Progressive venous distension over the upper chest and neck with constitutional symptoms in an older person with a smoking history strongly suggests superior vena cava obstruction due to an intrathoracic mass. In Australia, primary lung cancer is the leading cause, with central tumours and mediastinal lymphadenopathy commonly compressing the superior vena cava. The apparent obstruction of the inferior vena cava (with the epigastric vessels part of the collateral circulation) suggests that the obstruction is extending inferiorly.

Pancoast tumour is a lung carcinoma arising in the superior sulcus of the lung apex that can cause superior vena cava obstruction through direct invasion or compression by associated lymphadenopathy. In an older person who smokes with subacute progression of venous congestion in the upper chest and neck, constitutional symptoms, and the characteristic symptom of facial fullness when bending forward, this is the most likely diagnosis.

Budd-Chiari syndrome causes hepatic venous outflow obstruction leading to tender hepatomegaly, right upper quadrant pain, ascites and development of abdominal wall collaterals that drain cranially; it does not typically produce marked venous distension confined to the upper chest and neck, and this patient lacks abdominal findings.

Cirrhosis can cause portal hypertension with caput medusae around the umbilicus and stigmata such as spider naevi, palmar erythema and ascites; the venous prominence would be centred on the abdomen rather than the upper chest and neck, and there are no examination findings of chronic liver disease.

Emphysema is a chronic obstructive lung disease that causes dyspnoea, barrel chest, and hyperinflation but does not cause superior vena cava obstruction or the pattern of venous distension seen in this patient; whilst this patient has a smoking history that predisposes to emphysema, the clinical presentation is not consistent with this diagnosis.

Thymoma is a possible anterior mediastinal tumour that can compress mediastinal structures, including the superior vena cava, but is less common than lung cancer in this age group and risk profile; without associated features such as myasthenic symptoms, it is a less likely cause compared with lung carcinoma.
learningPoints: Progressive upper chest venous distension with weight loss suggests superior vena cava obstruction, most commonly from lung cancer in smokers.
Facial fullness when bending forward is a classic symptom of superior vena cava syndrome due to impaired venous drainage.
Pancoast tumours at the lung apex can cause superior vena cava obstruction through direct invasion or lymph node involvement.
references: Nasim F, Sabath BF, Wapen GA. Lung Cancer. Med Clin N Am 2019; 103:463–73
Wright K, Digby GC, Gyawali B, et al. Malignant Superior Vena Cava Syndrome: A Scoping Review. J Thorac Oncol. 2023; 10:1268-276.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
